pattern: In a functional graph every node leads into at most one cycle, so timestamping nodes along each walk lets a revisit on the current path reveal a cycle's length directly.
hints: Each node has at most one successor, so any walk eventually either dies at -1 or loops; a loop is the cycle to measure.
Stamp each node with the step at which you first see it on the current walk; if you revisit a node from the same walk, the step difference is the cycle length.
Track a global visited set and per-walk first-seen timestamps; when you re-enter a node visited on this same walk, the current step minus its stamp is a cycle length, and you take the maximum.

edge_inputs.description: self-loop disallowed so a two-node cycle is minimal
tail feeding into a cycle must not inflate the length
two disjoint cycles, the longer one wins

stress.description: random functional graph mixing cycles and dead ends
stress.note: edges[i] != i enforced; many small cycles plus tails

oracle_python:
class Solution:
    def longestCycle(self, edges):
        # Independent: classic Tarjan-style iterative DFS coloring, measuring the
        # back-edge depth difference when a gray node is re-encountered.
        n = len(edges)
        color = [0] * n  # 0 white, 1 gray, 2 black
        depth = [0] * n
        best = -1
        for s in range(n):
            if color[s] != 0:
                continue
            path = []
            cur = s
            d = 0
            while cur != -1 and color[cur] == 0:
                color[cur] = 1
                depth[cur] = d
                path.append(cur)
                d += 1
                cur = edges[cur]
            if cur != -1 and color[cur] == 1:
                best = max(best, d - depth[cur])
            for node in path:
                color[node] = 2
        return best
